one_line_takeaway: The first survey to frame zero-trust ('never trust, always verify', NIST SP 800-207) for multi-LLM agentic systems at the edge, contributing an intra-/inter-LLM threat taxonomy, a conceptual seven-module zero-trust reference architecture, and a model-level vs system-level mechanism taxonomy -- but with zero original experiments or measured validation.
key_finding: The central thesis: traditional perimeter-based defenses for LLMs -- adversarial training, differential privacy, RLHF, TEEs/hardware enclaves, reactive output filtering, access-control/firewalls, secure communication protocols -- meaningfully improve trustworthiness but are structurally inadequate for dynamic multi-LLM EGI, because security boundaries are hard to define as capabilities grow, defenses are reactive (transient malicious content still leaks), and collaboration enables lateral movement across domains. Zero-trust (explicit verification, least privilege, continuous monitoring, micro-segmentation) is argued to be the appropriate paradigm, and the authors show it can be systematically realized only as end-to-end systems engineering coordinating both model-level and system-level mechanisms -- partial adoption (e.g., identity verification without continuous monitoring) can create serious residual vulnerabilities.
core_contribution: A survey/tutorial (ACM Computing Surveys) that the authors claim is the first systematic treatment of applying the zero-trust security paradigm ('never trust, always verify') to multi-LLM agentic systems in Edge General Intelligence (EGI). The paper's OWN contributions (as distinct from the ~150 works it reviews) are: (1) a taxonomy of security issues for multi-LLM EGI split into intra-LLM threats (jailbreak/prompt injection, unpredictable emergent abilities, data leakage/privacy, toxic/misaligned responses) and inter-LLM threats (expanded attack surface, over-permissive integration, insecure inter-LLM communication, Byzantine consensus manipulation, cross-context data leakage) -- Table 2; (2) a proposed unified zero-trust multi-LLM reference architecture instantiating NIST SP 800-207, built from seven modules (mobile-edge LLMs, cloud LLM policy engine, identity/authentication, inter-LLM communication gateway, user-input checking, multi-layer output verification, behavioral auditing/anomaly detection) plus a five-step end-to-end operational workflow, illustrated with an autonomous-driving case study (Fig. 4); (3) a two-axis taxonomy of zero-trust mechanisms into model/intra-LLM level (Table 4: strong identity/authentication/authorization, context-aware access control, stateless & ephemeral LLM management) and system/inter-LLM level (Table 5: proactive maintenance, blockchain/distributed management, micro-segmentation & isolation, intelligent monitoring); (4) a structured comparison of perimeter-based vs zero-trust security across philosophy, enablers, overhead, target attacks (Table 3); (5) a comparison of prior related surveys along Single-LLM/Multi-LLM/EGI/Zero-Trust coverage (Table 1); and (6) three future research directions.
threat_model: Adversaries against collaborative multi-LLM systems deployed across cloud, edge servers/roadside units, and on-device chips in EGI (e.g., autonomous driving, smart healthcare, smart grids). The paper rejects implicit trust of any component: attackers may be external (network intrusion, prompt injection at the user boundary) OR an insider/compromised LLM node acting maliciously (Byzantine). Enumerated threats: jailbreak and prompt-injection to bypass safety guardrails; 'prompt infection' where a malicious payload cascades covertly between LLMs via normal message passing (chain-of-compromise); a single rogue LLM poisoning the decision pipeline or manipulating consensus (dishonest voting, collusion, or a compromised coordinator); insecure inter-LLM channels enabling impersonation/Sybil attacks, eavesdropping, and traffic reconstruction; over-permissive tool/API integration enabling privilege escalation and unintended physical actions (cites the GPT-4 TaskRabbit CAPTCHA-bypass anecdote); cross-context data leakage when policy-compliant LLMs combine to violate global privacy constraints. The core argument is that perimeter-based defenses fail here because boundaries are ill-defined (rapidly expanding LLM capabilities), defenses are reactive, and dense collaboration creates lateral-movement opportunities.
limitations: As a survey the paper offers no empirical validation: the proposed unified zero-trust multi-LLM architecture is a conceptual vision/reference design, never implemented, measured, or compared against baselines. The authors themselves acknowledge (Table 3) that zero-trust incurs high operational overhead -- continuous authentication, real-time monitoring, dynamic permission management, multi-system coordination -- and is impractical for resource-constrained edge under some conditions, but do not quantify this tradeoff. All performance numbers in the text come from cited works, so the survey provides no independent evidence that the assembled mechanisms compose effectively or that the claimed 'first' framework outperforms perimeter approaches. Coverage is arXiv/2025-vintage and the scope (EGI + multi-LLM + zero-trust) is narrow enough that generalization beyond the autonomous-driving illustration is asserted rather than demonstrated.

quantitative_results: No original/measured results -- this is a survey. All figures below are quoted from the works it cites, not produced by the authors.
[cited, AWS Bedrock blog] dynamic multi-LLM routing reduces response latency by up to 50% and operational costs by 30%.
[cited ref 63] GPT-4 can autonomously generate functional exploit code for 87% of known one-day software vulnerabilities given only their official descriptions.
[cited, Sanyal et al. ref 87] across 16 state-of-the-art LLMs and 40 enterprise scenarios, even GPT-4.1 achieves only 27% accuracy when reasoning about complex multi-permission (RBAC) cases.
[cited, Chrapek et al. ref 74] running LLMs inside Intel SGX/TDX hardware enclaves incurs less than 10% overhead versus conventional insecure deployment.
[cited, system-level input-checking work, Sec 6] a proactive intelligent input-checking method detects 97% of various prompt-injection attacks.
[cited] Self-Defense output filtering reportedly reduces adversarial-prompt attack success rate to virtually zero (qualitative, no single number given in this survey).
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines (no experiments). Conceptual comparisons only:
Table 3: perimeter-based/trustworthy security vs zero-trust security for multi-LLM (philosophy, trust assumption, enablers, training/operational overhead, target attacks, advantages, limitations).
Table 1: prior related surveys contrasted by coverage of Single-LLM / Multi-LLM / EGI / Zero-Trust (refs 18-25).

Surveyed perimeter techniques treated as the paradigm being superseded: adversarial training (CAT, ReFAT, AdversaFlow), differential privacy, RLHF (Safe RLHF), TEEs/hardware enclaves (SGX/TDX, LoRATEE), reactive output filtering (Self-Defense), access control & firewalls (Progent RBAC, FirewaLLM, ControlNet), secure communication protocols (MCP-Shield, MCIP).
